content.heading: Result<T, E>
content.lead: The foundation of Rust's error handling. There is no try/catch ; errors are values that your functions return explicitly.
content.intro: In JavaScript, errors are thrown as exceptions. In Rust, errors are values that you handle like any other data. A function either succeeds with an Ok(value) or fails with an Err(error). This forces you to think about failure cases upfront.

sections.text: The Result enum ; two outcomes
Any function that can fail returns Result<T, E>. T is the success type, E is the error type.
Comparing to JavaScript

left.label: JavaScript try/catch

right.label: Rust Result

sections.text: Three ways to handle Result
1. The ? operator (most common)
2. Pattern matching (when you need custom handling)
3. Methods on Result

rows: In tests and when you're certain it's Ok
Get value or panic with message
Prototyping, or when panic is acceptable
When you have a sensible fallback

sections.text: Box<dyn Error> ; accepting any error type
In vaultlog, your functions will return this:

sections.head: 🎯 Why Box<dyn Error>?
sections.text: Box<dyn Error> is a "trait object" that can hold any error type ; io::Error, serde_json::Error, parse errors, etc. The ? operator automatically converts them. This is the pragmatic choice for beginners: "I don't know what error types I'll encounter, just give me a box that holds them all."

content.heading: The ? Operator
content.lead: The single most important syntax in Rust error handling. It means: "If this returns Err, immediately return that error from my function. If it's Ok, unwrap and continue."

sections.text: ? is syntactic sugar for early return
Chaining ? calls
This is where ? really shines. You can chain operations without nesting:
Notice there's no nesting or callback hell. Each ? either succeeds and continues, or returns an error. Clean control flow.
? only works in functions that return Result (or Option)
Converting error types with ?
The ? operator automatically calls .into() on the error to convert it to the return type. This is why Box<dyn Error> works so well ; all standard errors implement the conversion:

sections.head: ⚠ The ? operator consumes the error
sections.text: Once you use ?, the error is converted and propagated ; you can't inspect it. If you need to handle the error differently based on its type, use match instead.

content.heading: Pattern Matching
content.lead: Rust's match is a supercharged switch statement. It's exhaustive ; the compiler forces you to handle every possible case.

sections.text: Basic match

left.label: JavaScript switch (fallthrough)

right.label: Rust match (exhaustive, no fallthrough)

sections.text: Matching enums ; extracting data
This is where match becomes incredibly powerful. Each enum variant can hold different data, and match lets you extract it:
Matching on Option<T>
Matching on Result<T, E>
if let ; single-case match
When you only care about one pattern, if let is cleaner:
while let ; looping until None
Guard clauses ; matching with conditions

sections.head: 🎯 Exhaustiveness checking
sections.text: Rust's compiler checks that your match covers all possible cases. If you add a new enum variant, the compiler tells you everywhere you need to update your match statements. This prevents silent bugs where you forget to handle a new case.

content.heading: Closures & Iterators
content.lead: Rust's closures and iterators are nearly identical to JavaScript's ; with one key difference: Rust iterators are lazy and need .collect() to run.

sections.text: Closures ; like arrow functions

left.label: JavaScript

right.label: Rust

sections.text: Iterator adapters are lazy
In JavaScript, .map() runs immediately. In Rust, it builds a pipeline that doesn't execute until you call .collect():
Common iterator methods
filter_map ; the power pattern
In vaultlog's storage layer, you'll read entries as JSON lines and skip any that fail to parse:
filter_map takes a closure that returns Option ; Some(value) is kept, None is discarded. Perfect for parsing and filtering in one step.
Closure borrowing ; move vs borrow

sections.head: 💡 Remember .collect()
sections.text: Iterators in Rust are lazy ; they're a sequence of transformations that hasn't run yet. Forgetting .collect() is one of the most common iterator mistakes. The compiler will tell you if a variable is unused, which helps catch this.

content.heading: Complete vaultlog CLI
content.lead: Here's the full working CLI that ties together everything from Phase 1 ; error handling, pattern matching, closures, iterators, and Clap.

sections.text: Project setup

cargo new vaultlog
cd vaultlog
Update Cargo.toml:
src/models.rs ; the data structures
src/ledger.rs ; hashing and verification
src/storage.rs ; file I/O with iterators
src/main.rs ; complete CLI with Clap
Running it

cargo run -- add --description "Purchased 123 Main St" --category asset
cargo run -- add --description "Inspection completed" --category inspection
cargo run -- list
cargo run -- verify

sections.head: 💡 What you've built
sections.text: This is a complete, working CLI tool with proper error handling, file I/O, JSON serialization, command parsing, and hash chain verification. Every concept maps directly to the larger REAL project and the Axum API you'll build next.

content.heading: async/await in Rust
content.lead: Rust's async/await looks similar to JavaScript's, but it's built on a different foundation. Understanding this difference is crucial for web development.

sections.text: JS async is built on promises
Rust async needs a runtime
Rust's async/await is lazy ; it doesn't run until a runtime (like Tokio) executes it. This gives you fine-grained control at the cost of explicitness:
async fn returns a Future
In Rust, async fn doesn't execute immediately ; it returns a Future, which is a lazy value that does something when awaited:
Tokio tasks ; lightweight concurrency
In Tokio, you spawn tasks instead of threads. Tasks are much cheaper:

sections.head: 🎯 Key difference from JS
sections.text: JS async: automatically runs with the event loop. Rust async: lazy, needs a runtime (Tokio) to execute. You have explicit control ; tasks don't start until spawned or awaited.

content.heading: Axum Basics
content.lead: Axum is a web framework built on Tokio. Handlers are async functions that return JSON, HTML, or custom responses. Routing is composed from handlers.

sections.text: A minimal Axum server
Handlers are just async functions
A handler takes extractors as parameters and returns a response. Axum handles serialization/deserialization automatically:
Routing ; connecting handlers to paths

sections.head: 🎯 Handler anatomy
sections.text: A handler is: 1. An async fn. 2. Takes zero or more extractors as parameters. 3. Returns a response type (Json, Html, String, custom Response). Axum automatically deserializes request data into extractors and serializes return types into HTTP responses.

content.heading: Extractors
content.lead: Extractors are how handlers receive data from HTTP requests. Axum provides built-in extractors for JSON, path params, query strings, headers, and more.

sections.text: The main extractors
Json extractor
Path extractor
Query extractor
Combining extractors

content.heading: Shared State
content.lead: In Axum, handlers run concurrently. To safely share data between them, you need to understand Arc (Atomic Reference Counting) and Mutex (Mutual Exclusion).

sections.text: The problem: multiple handlers, one database
Arc<T> ; shared ownership
Arc stands for "Atomic Reference Counting". It lets multiple owners share the same value:
Mutex<T> ; safe concurrent access
Mutex ensures only one task can access the data at a time:
Arc<Mutex<T>> ; shared mutable state
Combine Arc and Mutex to share mutable data across async tasks:

sections.head: ⚠ Holding locks across .await
sections.text: Never hold a Mutex lock while awaiting. The lock should be acquired, modified, and released within a single synchronous section. Holding a lock across async boundaries can cause deadlocks.

content.heading: CLI → API
content.lead: Converting vaultlog from a CLI to a REST API. The business logic stays the same ; you're just swapping the interface from command-line to HTTP.

sections.text: The conversion
Axum API server for vaultlog
Testing the API

sections.head: 💡 Business logic stays the same
sections.text: Your ledger.rs and models.rs from Phase 1 don't change at all. You're just wrapping them with HTTP handlers. This makes it easy to test core logic independently of the web layer.

content.heading: sqlx Basics
content.lead: sqlx is an async SQL library with compile-time query checking. You write SQL, and sqlx verifies it against your database schema at compile time.

sections.text: Setup with Supabase
Add to Cargo.toml:
Create .env:
DATABASE_URL=postgresql://user:password@host:5432/database
In Supabase, get your connection string from the "Connect" button in your project dashboard.
Connecting to the database
Running a simple query
Querying into structs
Parameterized queries (preventing SQL injection)
The $1, $2, etc. are placeholders. .bind() safely parameterizes the query.

sections.head: 🎯 Compile-time checking
sections.text: If you run cargo build with DATABASE_URL set and the database online, sqlx will verify your queries at compile time. Typos in column names and type mismatches become compile errors, not runtime panics.

content.heading: Query Building
content.lead: Common patterns for inserting, updating, and fetching data. Every query is parameterized to prevent SQL injection.

sections.text: Insert ; adding an entry
Select with filtering
Update
Transaction ; multiple queries atomically

sections.head: 💡 Always use parameters ($1, $2, etc.)
sections.text: Never string-interpolate user input into SQL. Always use .bind() to pass parameters safely.

content.heading: Migrations
content.lead: Migrations are versioned SQL files that define your schema. sqlx runs them in order, ensuring your database stays in sync with your code.

sections.text: Setting up migrations
This creates migrations/ directory with files like:

migrations/
├── 20240101120000_create_log_entries.up.sql
└── 20240101120000_create_log_entries.down.sql
Migration files for vaultlog
migrations/20240101120000_create_log_entries.up.sql:
migrations/20240101120000_create_log_entries.down.sql (rollback):
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS log_entries CASCADE;
Running migrations
Creating additional migrations

sections.head: 🎯 Migrations + Version Control
sections.text: Commit migrations to git. Each migration is versioned. New developers or CI/CD can run sqlx migrate run to get the exact schema your code expects. No more "script to create the database" chaos.

content.heading: Full Integration
content.lead: Bringing it all together: Axum API + sqlx queries + Supabase database. The complete vaultlog REST API backed by a real database.

sections.text: Project structure

vaultlog/
├── Cargo.toml
├── .env
├── .sqlx
├── migrations/
│   └── 20240101120000_create_log_entries.up.sql
├── src/
│   ├── main.rs
│   ├── models.rs
│   ├── ledger.rs
│   ├── handlers.rs      ← NEW
│   └── db.rs            ← NEW
src/models.rs ; reuse from Phase 1
src/db.rs ; database operations
src/main.rs ; tying it all together
Testing the complete API

sections.head: 🎯 You now have
sections.text:
• A working REST API (Axum)
• Type-safe database queries (sqlx)
• Versioned schema with migrations
• All the core concepts: ownership, async, error handling, pattern matching
• A real product ready to expand
Where to go from here

items: Authentication: Add JWT or session tokens with middleware
Validation: Use validator crate to validate requests
Logging: Add tracing for structured logs
Testing: Write unit tests for handlers and db functions
Deployment: Docker, Fly.io, Railway, or Vercel for serverless
Frontend: React/Next.js calling your REST API
